title: HTTP/1.1 Message Framing and Length Precedence · Go Scenario Checkpoint
summary: A security review compares near-miss parser decisions for http/1.1 message framing and length precedence and requires the smallest strict RFC 9112 message-body length decision table with changed-input evidence. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for RFC 9112 message-body length decision table. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Apply the RFC 9112 message-body length precedence in order using request or response role, method, status, transfer coding, content length, and connection closure.
Forbid content framing for HEAD responses, 1xx, 204, successful CONNECT, and other context-specific cases while retaining metadata meaning.
Validate transfer-coding order, require final chunked coding for request framing, and close after ambiguous Transfer-Encoding plus Content-Length input.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Diagnose the hidden failure · Quiz Framing Completeness Evidence
steps.instruction:
quiz case — A quiz protocol team handles case 2407 in a partner TLS endpoint negotiating HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 through ALPN. Build deterministic Go evidence to apply framing precedence and close on Transfer-Encoding plus Content-Length ambiguity; preserve this operating constraint: the browser model cannot open a socket or claim packet-level proof; then append a second pipelined message and prove the first parser leaves the exact residual bytes. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Record framing source, expected bytes, observed bytes, completion, residual buffer, close requirement, and rejection reason.

The visible result looks correct, but its invariant or value contract is unverified. What must happen next?

Evidence target: Diagnose the hidden failure · Quiz Framing Completeness Evidence.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for record framing source, expected bytes, observed bytes, completion, residual buffer, close requirement, and rejection reason. For Diagnose the hidden failure · Quiz Framing Completeness Evidence, keep Framing Completeness Evidence connected to executable Go behavior evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Record framing source, expected bytes, observed bytes, completion, residual buffer, close requirement, and rejection reason.

hints: For Defend one difficult decision · Quiz Framing Completeness Evidence, name the types, ownership, and stop path, Framing Completeness Evidence precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Defend one difficult decision · Quiz Framing Completeness Evidence, trace this concrete input and predict values, errors, blocking, and output before reviewing it: A quiz protocol team handles case 2414 in a field-normalizing cache with an independent origin parser. Build deterministic Go evidence to apply framing precedence and close on Transfer-Encoding plus Content-Length ambiguity; preserve this operating constraint: the browser model cannot open a socket or claim packet-level proof; then replace one field with repeated non-combinable lines and preserve their order. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Record framing source, expected bytes, observed bytes, completion, residual buffer, close requirement, and rejection reason.

For Defend one difficult decision · Quiz Framing Completeness Evidence, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, failure, race, or toolchain evidence: A valid start line and field section prove the complete message is safe to dispatch.

checks.description: The evidence cycle runs from problem framing through changed-case validation.
checks.failureMessage: Frame the decision before modeling, and validate after obtaining a result.

checks.description: The evidence note explains the model, failure risk, and verification in the learner’s own words.
checks.failureMessage: Add a causal explanation using record and framing plus specific case evidence.
